speaker: interviewer
text: Mr. Weinreich, can you continue and tell us a little bit some of the experiences that you had in Kyzylorda.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. In Kyzylorda I was going to school. In the technical college, I was going from three o'clock late. And when I moved to the city, from eight o'clock in the morning till twelve I was going to school. From twelve, I had a bit of a break. And then there was a couple of boys in there, a couple of brothers, which came originally from Lodz, and they made what they call a [NON-ENGLISH], you know, to make cloth. And I was the one which was sitting on the [NON-ENGLISH] and was making the cloth.

speaker: interviewer
text: On a loom?

speaker: interviewee
text: On a loom. And I was very good at it. And my mother was wrapping it up around her body and she was going to the market and sell them on the black market. So that was a few hours a day.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then at eight o'clock in the evening, I was an apprentice in a newspaper, you know, printing newspapers. And I remember one little accident, which I'll go into very quickly. I didn't like-- you see, when you had the paper ready, it was only four pages, right? So you had to put it in a machine, you know, and screw it up, and then the machine was going with the paper, right? And you couldn't stop printing until the men from [INAUDIBLE] came and approved it.

speaker: interviewee
text: And one day, you know, when it was approved, you know, was one thing written, you know, one thing printed, and you looked at it. And I didn't like the lies which was in there on that particular day, so I made it loose. So the machine started, the whole thing flew out the window.

speaker: interviewer
text: Anyway, so I was working-- it was three jobs. School, loom, and what's the name? And that was going on for quite a long time on three jobs. Three things. And so that's how my life was there.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, the things which I remember very clearly in there is this. There was a few incidents which I'll tell you very quickly because we have to move on. Number one, my mother was working in a factory to make clothing for the army. And later on my uncle became one of the chief accountants in the rice factory. He was a brilliant man. The man was brilliant. And so he fixed up a job for my mother in the rice factory. So, first of all, it was not so hard. The job was not so hard. And, second, she could steal a little rice. So she was working in there too.

speaker: interviewer
text: And, so what I remember about the rice factory was this. Was about, I would say, 400 Jewish people was working in there. Rosh Hashanah, Yom Kippur comes. Nobody wants to go to work. But how can you get out of it? You've got to have a [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH] from a doctor that you are dying. You're sick, so sick, that you can't go to work. There was a Russian Jewish doctor, and he gave everybody a [NON-ENGLISH] that they're sick, right? Of course, his job was on the line and nobody would have believed him, but they shut their eyes. Next to us where we lived was a little rabbi, you know? And, you know, in Kazakhstan, there was, as I told you, squares. You know, you walked and there's a door, and there's a house this way, a house this way, and a house this way, you know? And you close the door, you don't know what's going on inside, right? So everybody ran in there to the rabbi for prayers. And we were there for prayers. All of a sudden-- the afternoon was Yom Kippur afternoon-- two truckloads of the secret police arrived. They see it all and they run inside. They break the door, run inside.

speaker: interviewee
text: And at that time was a decree, only three people could meet. If more than three people you've got to have a-- they were worried about some sort of a conspiracy.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what are you doing in here? So many people. Was about 100 people in there, you know, all praying. Tried to explain that this is a holiday. What holiday? It was a [INAUDIBLE]. Society didn't know about all this.

speaker: interviewee
text: They took us all, including me, into the station. We come to the station. The chief of the police in Kazakhstan, in the [INAUDIBLE], was a Jewish man called Miller. I'll never forget him.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Miller. And he was small, and his bodyguard was twice his size, right? Always running around with his bodyguard.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he started to scream. Every minute of the day, our brothers and sisters are being killed on the front, and you are going to pray? He said, there's no God. There's nobody like that. Now get the hell out of here. I don't want to see you anymore. And go back to work or go back to your home or whatever.

speaker: interviewee
text: We all went back to the rabbi, to make it to [INAUDIBLE] service. Miller arrived out of his uniform, in civil clothes, and he said Kaddish. I could never in my life believe it. Was a very tragic story with Miller. He had a son, a young son, and he was a lieutenant in the army. And his group was one of the first to break through in Berlin. And, actually, he was killed in the Reichstag, and the whole city was weeping. This is what I remember in Kyzylorda, this one thing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Next thing what I remember Kyzylorda is this. I was working on the [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH], you know, on the loom, and my mother was going to the market and she was also buying a bit of rice and selling a bit of rice to make a bit of money, you know, when the war is finished, that way when we go home we'll have something to start with, right?

speaker: interviewee
text: And one day she comes home and she said to me, there's a man I met in the market, a Jewish man I met in the market, and he's giving me a tremendous good rate to buy [NON-ENGLISH]. [NON-ENGLISH] was American gold dollars. And so, she got all together all the money which she had at home, and she went to a couple of the neighbors to borrow some more money and she went to the market. She came to the market and she came home. She was very happy that she's such a good deal. And the money was in a tube. And she-- I'll never forget it. She pulls out the tube on a table there, you know,a little table. So the first three was gold coins and the rest was [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: And I knew that there's a train going to Tashkent and I'll catch the fellow. I said, how does he look? So she described him. I run to the train, and I felt this, that this is the fellow.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I said to him in Hebrew, I said, [SPEAKING HEBREW]. I said, you'll die together with me. I said, you don't give back my mother the money, or, I said, you'll finish up in jail. We'll finish up in jail. You're not allowed to sell, I'm not allowed to buy.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he said, OK, let's go outside. And he run between the trains and I couldn't catch him. That was it. And then, when I came home, I'll never forget it. My mother was in utter despair, not because she lost her money, but because she borrowed the money from her neighbors. Three days later, I'm going to the post office. You know, we were going to the post office, you know, to see what any letters arrived, whatever, right? Two parcels arrived from Warsaw.

speaker: interviewee
text: I had a cousin which was an army officer, and he was stealing some stuff there in Warsaw from I don't know who from and he sent us. It was clothes, suits, shoes, socks, shirts. Two parcels. I went to the market together with mother, we sold it, and we repaid everybody.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I've got a photo of this fellow, you know? I've got it here. Because he lived in Australia. That's the second thing. The third thing which I remember which was very interesting was, in school, when I was going to school, that year, nine, I had three classes, because it was such an overflow of kids. Three classes a night. And I was in the highest of the three classes, you know? The brightest kids were in that class, the not so bright in this, and the dumb ones in there. So I was in the highest class. Next to me was sitting a Kazakh, and a very fine fellow. Tall, fine fellow. And he used to take me to his home.

speaker: interviewee
text: And, you know, I just wanted to give you a quick description of the home. You know, they had a-- you know, a mud home, a mud home, and very ingenious. You know, was a hole in the floor, a hole, and they used a little bit of wood, but it was going right around the floor into the walls and out in the ceiling. So with a little bit of wood, they had a warm house. Because in wintertime, it was bitterly cold in there. There was no snow, but it was bitterly cold. You know, windy.

speaker: interviewer
text: And one day, the director of the school comes into the class with one man from the [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH] and he said, listen, boys. We know that this is the more intellectual class of the three, and we give you a wonderful opportunity. They put on three sentences on the blackboard. You will answer it. You've got two hours to answer it, either one of those political things. And then we're given opportunity to three boys to go to Moscow to go into diplomatic corps. All right?

speaker: interviewee
text: And I was very interested in politics, always very interested in politics. Not only that, but I used to get up at six o'clock in the morning and there was a huge map in the corridor, and I was the one who's telling anybody what's happening in the war. You know, and I always remember the fellow which was on the radio was saying, [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH]. You know, our troops have today, you know, occupied such and such a city. And I used to, with flags, I used to show them the troop movements of the Russians and Germans and so on. Very interested in that. I was very interested.

speaker: interviewer
text: And, anyway, after two days later, the director calls in my friend which was sitting next to me, me, and another fellow, and he said, you three were privileged, you know, and you are going to go to Moscow to a diplomatic school. I said I would like to go very much, but I'm not a Russian citizen. Right? I'm a Polish citizen. Oh, we can't have you. Right? We don't-- only Russians. So they left me behind. A year later, I walk down the street and I see this fellow beautifully dressed in a beautiful uniform with gold epaulets. Yosef! [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH] You know? I said yes. Oh! My friend the Kazakh. I Said, let's go to the china, you know, the tea. Let's have a cup of tea. And his first words to me were like this, [SPEAKING HEBREW] Do you speak Hebrew? In Hebrew! And I said, what the hell happened to you? So he told me they took him to Moscow, into diplomatico, into the school, and they had a professor of some kind of Hebrew, and they were teaching them. I said, what do you need Hebrew for? He said, look. The war's going to finish very shortly, right, and the Jews are communists, as you know, because we've got all the [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH]-- Russian Jews are very good communists.

speaker: interviewee
text: We are going to establish-- we are going to help you to establish a Jewish state in Palestine, and it'll be the same as in Poland. We're going to set up a communist state in there, and this will be the springboard for Africa and India. And all our diplomats, wherever they go, they know the language of the country, and they know the history of the country better than the natives. And because I have got-- because the Kazakh had a guttural voice. They spoke from inside. So they put me into the Hebrew section. [SPEAKING HEBREW] And he was speaking Hebrew.

speaker: interviewer
text: It was just an amazing story, you know? I could not believe it. I'm positive that when what's the name started, you know, when the Russian embassy was-- consulate was in Tel Aviv, he must have been one of them. It's fascinating. Quite remarkable.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Very remarkable.

speaker: interviewee
text: It's a remarkable story. So that's the second story which I remember from Kyzylorda. These are the stories which come to my mind.

speaker: interviewer
text: So for you living there, you were able to earn a living with your mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, but we lost everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Were you able to then put together more money again and--

speaker: interviewee
text: A little bit. A little bit more money by selling the clothes which my cousin sent. A little, but not much.

speaker: interviewer
text: You seem to have managed to keep in touch with what was going on in the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yes. Oh yes. You know, I was always-- I loved politics. And Mr. Wulkan, because he taught me Hebrew and he taught me Yiddish and so on, I had to read the Russian newspaper, what Churchill said and what Roosevelt said, and translate them in Yiddish.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your family stay healthy during this point in time?

speaker: interviewee
text: My family stayed-- no. No. You see, now we're coming to a stage where it was the most bitter time for my life. First of all, we were very hungry. That was for sure. You know, we're hungry, but it was bearable. We were not more hungry then the Russians. Because everything was going to the army, right, so you could excuse them. Now, the only thing which happened was that, in Kazakhstan, unfortunately, you know, because of the climate, was warm and so on, typhus was raging. It was an epidemic. And I was one of the first to get typhus. There was two types of typhuses. Came from the stomach and from lice. And I got sick. I can't remember which one was first, but I got sick. And I was taken to the hospital. There was absolutely no medication. And the doctor said I'm not going to live. I'm not going to survive. So I did survive, without any medication, without anything. You know, with aspirin or something, whatever they gave me there. And as soon as I got out of that first, you know, typhus, imagine, I got the next one. So in 1944, I had two typhuses. I was sick twice on typhus, and on both occasions, I was not supposed to be alive. And I went through-- that was the most bitterest time of my life was '44.

speaker: interviewer
text: And, therefore, I want to tell my children, and they know it very well, that my philosophy in life has always been-- since then, 1944, it's not. No matter what happens in life. So I always say to myself, 1944, it's not. If I overcome that, I can overcome this. This is peanuts against what happened there, right? And then, unfortunately, my beloved uncle, Abraham Blumenfrucht, got sick on typhus. And because I had typhus twice, so I was regarded as I'm immune, and we bribed the staff in the hospital-- if you could call it a hospital because there was bed one next to the other. There was no room to go in between, right? And the professor was a Jewish man, and he was so poor he was wearing a woman's coat.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I was lying next to my uncle for 10 days and he died in my hands. A young man, brilliant young man, with a brilliant mind. And he was to me not like an uncle, like a father. Because I didn't have my father. My father was-- you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that was very bitter. That was the worst time in my life. After two typhuses, which I went through, you know, Mr. Wulkan's son died on typhus. It was such a tragedy in there, I can't even tell you. He had three daughters and a son, and the youngest was the boy. A brilliant young man. Also died. Thousands upon thousands of our people died. It caught you, 10 days, finished. You're very lucky to survive.

speaker: interviewee
text: Very lucky to survive. And that's how I survived in Kyzylorda.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you stayed in Kyzylorda until when?

speaker: interviewee
text: The war finished. As a matter of fact, you know, we're doing this interview the 5th of May. That's when the war finished, on the 5th of May. How many years ago? It's, what, 51 years ago. 51 years ago to the day. Today we're making this interview.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the end of the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very well. I remember, you know, the radios were full blast. Everybody had their radios full blast. [NON-ENGLISH]. Peace, right? [NON-ENGLISH]. You know, the war's finished. And there was screaming from everywhere. [SPEAKING HEBREW]. Our cause is right, right? We was victorious. We're victorious. And Stalin at that time did not say [RUSSIAN], to go to fight for Stalin. [RUSSIAN]. For your country. And, you know, the atmosphere was electric. 24 hours the people were dancing in the streets. There was non stop. There was music going from everywhere, anywhere. And there was the Kazakhis and this and that, you know. All the dancers, the Russian folk dancers. And it was drunkenness, and it was-- but it was in a jovial mood. It was colossal. It was colossal. You know.

speaker: interviewer
text: But, soon after the war was finished, and so forth, there was a fear in Russia, a colossal fear. I don't know. The world doesn't realize it, what a fear it was. The Americans dropped the bombs on Nagasaki and Hiroshima, and they were frightened that the next load would be on Moscow, Leningrad, because they knew that they hate the communists, you know? So there was, straight away, after that mood of happiness and joy, there was a mood of fear. Because, at that time, Russia didn't have the atom bomb yet. What did you decide to do with your mother at that stage?

speaker: interviewee
text: At that stage, we couldn't do anything. We just stayed where we are. And then they came to us and they-- not came to us, but they announced, [INAUDIBLE], who-- and that is where our thoughts and [NON-ENGLISH] was right. Whoever is not a Russian citizen cannot stay in Russia and cannot stay in Kyzylorda, but you have to go and register and go back to where you come from. And that's where we're justified not to take the Russian citizenship.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it took awhile before-- the war finished in '45, and we were given permission to go back to Poland. Not permission, but we were allocated room. It was cattle trains again, you know, in '46. Did you know the fate of the rest of your family at that stage?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. We had no idea.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you went in '46 back to--

speaker: interviewee
text: Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the journey took us four weeks. Again, I was a cook level. You see, there was a commandant there which was organizing, you know, food and so on. I was his helper. And I was wearing a pajama which my mother made. You know, I was weaving it and my mother was sewing it. And after I got married, I took it home, and the second or third day after my marriage my wife thought it was a piece of schmuk and so she threw it out. Anyway, so we went back to Poland. From Kyzylorda we went back to Poland. There's one thing I want to tell you what happened on the way, you know. One little thing. Two things, actually, to give you some idea what was going on. First of all, there was a trainload of 500 people again. Somehow we always had trainloads of 500 people. So many wagons and so many people in wagons. So again, everybody was on lying on the floor, on a bit of straw. And I was, you know, I was always, you know, thinking. And they said, in Poland, there's a lake of salt-- salt is like gold, and also rice.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we went-- in Kyzylorda was plenty of rice, and we bought some rice. And on the Caspian Sea, when we were passing the Caspian sea, we bought salt, because that's where the salt. And so I had two big bags, one rice, and one salt. A few schmattas. Really, a few schmattas.

speaker: interviewee
text: And on the way, we were going to west Poland. And, of course, the trains with troops were coming back.

speaker: interviewer
text: One more thing I forgot to tell you. In Kyzylorda I met, you know, when I was on the railway station, there was a passenger train, and out came German officers, and [INAUDIBLE] Paulus was there. And I saw [INAUDIBLE] Paulus. You know, that was the defender, the marshal, which was fighting for Stalingrad, right? Anyway, so, and trainloads of German troops which were coming back, you know, and they were chucking out the dead on the line, you know.

speaker: interviewee
text: And so, going back to Poland, on the way there was such misery that you have never seen in your life. Because a lot of people came out from concentration camps. Russian concentration, spies-- they were supposed to be spies, you know. And all sort of things. And they were sitting there on the lines when the train stopped and begging for food. And about a day before we arrived in Poland, we went again through Przemysl-- same city where, you know, when we went over the [INAUDIBLE]. Just a day or two days before we--

speaker: interviewer
text: So, I saw these people sitting in there in terrible conditions. Full of lice and on their shoes-- instead of shoes they had pieces of, you know, rubber, you know, from, you know, from the cars, you know, and so forth. And one fellow was looking up at me with such soulful eyes and he said to me in Polish, are you going back to Poland? [SPEAKING POLISH] Can you take me with you? I said, how can I take you with me? You got documents to go back to Poland? He said, no. So how can I take him? My mother said, take him.

speaker: interviewee
text: I gave him my hand and I grabbed him and I took him up, and the train started to move. My mother undressed him from top to bottom, threw out all his clothes, and everybody gave him something. I gave him trousers. I don't know what I had trousers, extra trousers. My friend without legs gave him a shirt, you know, like from a marina, you know, from a sailor. And, anyway, he's coming with us. OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: We come in the morning-- early in the morning, we're coming to the boarder. Until we saw the border, you know? Coming to the border. So, door opens up, an officer with two soldiers. Again with his guns. Who speaks Polish and Russian? I do. OK. We will call out the names of the people, and every name which we call out, you take the document and give it to me, and the person goes down. Fine. So I call out the names. It was about 30 people, 40 people inside. I knew that I've got this fellow. He's got no documents. What am I going to do with him?

speaker: interviewee
text: Anyway, so I pushed him towards the front. I said, I already said your name and your document. Get down. So he jumped down and he disappeared. Somebody was there. A sigh of relief.

speaker: interviewer
text: After everybody was down, he said now, we're to do the reverse. The documents and everybody goes up. So everybody went up, and he stays there. He said, and who is this fellow? I said, ah, look. He's a Pollack. He wanted to go to Poland. He's from the concentration camp. You know, he did nothing wrong. Thief or something.

speaker: interviewee
text: He went up to him, and I'll never forget that. He gave him, one, two, and he said, [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH]. You will never sail anymore the seas. He had the a shirt of a sailor. And he took him. And he said, you are lucky that we are not undoing your part of the train, because you're in the middle, you know, and it was hard for them to shuffle around, otherwise the whole train would have stopped here. You wouldn't cross the border.

speaker: interviewer
text: We crossed the border. Crossed the border.

speaker: interviewee
text: Why I'm telling you that story about this Pollack, because there is a sequel to it. Cross the border. And they said, you cannot stop anywhere. Nobody can get out of the train. The whole train goes to Silesia, to the part of Poland where Poland-- past Krakow, past Krapkowice, into the German part where the Poles-- You see, the Russians took a part of Poland from one side, and the Poles took a part of Germany from the other side. So that's where you're going to go because that's where we need the people. We don't need you in any others city.

speaker: interviewer
text: Again, I was very clever, right? And we arrive in Plaszow, in Krakow. You know, that's a Sabbot from Krakow. And I said, ah, you know my uncle died, right? So I was the man of the family. I was the one who made all the decisions. I was 19. Made all the decisions. I said, we're going to try to get out here. Because what are we going to do in Silesia? There's nobody there. And here there could be some Jews in the area. It's a big city. We want to go from here, want to go to either Palestine or we want to go to Israel, we want to go to Australia, you know? What are we going to do here?

speaker: interviewee
text: Anyway, the train stopped and I jumped down from the train, down, and there was sort of a bridge, and under the bridge a Polish fellow with a horse and a cart. And I said to him, I've got two women and two children and a little bit of luggage, and I'll pay anything you want to take us to Krakow. Well, fellow had to use money, you know, so yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: I went up to the train, and I-- can you imagine what hardship I had to get the two women down, and the two boys? Two boys was no problem. And that bit of luggage. I had a bag of rice and a bag of salt. Heavy like hell. Anyway, we got it down and we got it all down quietly, and we started to move to the city. He said, [POLISH]? Where do you want to go? Give me the address. I said, let's go and I'll tell you. I said, to tell you the truth, I don't know where to go. I've got no place where to go. So he said, it's quite obvious to me, he said, you're Jewish. I said, yes. He said, there is a house in Krakow which looks after the refugees for a few days until he gets himself sorted out. I said, that's where I want to go. And as we are going like this, it was starting to drizzle. It was raining. And like this. And a woman was sliding up to me. She said are you going to Krakow? I say, yes. Can I come with you? Because it's too dangerous for women on their own to go, to walk to Krakow. I said, of course. I look at her, and she looks at me, and she said to me-- and I look at her and I said, [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH]? You Jewish? [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH] means my folk, my people. She said, yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: I said, I'm going to ask you a very stupid question. I said, I've got a cousin which I believe has lived through the war. He was in the partisans. His name is Joseph Goldberger, and he's a dentist. Said, I know him, and I know where he lives.

speaker: interviewer
text: And she took us to my cousin's place, in the middle of the city, you know? I knock on the door and he looks at me and he comes out. He looks at me, who are you? I say, my name is Joseph Weinreich. My God, Father. Who are you with? Where are you from? You know, where are you coming from? I said, this very minute, we came from Russia. My mother and my auntie Mina and the two boys there.

speaker: interviewee
text: He run down like crazy, he took the luggage, put it in his [NON-ENGLISH], you know, down the bottom and he took us upstairs. First we had a shower. Then he gave us something to eat. Then we'd all lie down on the floor. Next day, I went with him and you found one room in [INAUDIBLE], somewhere there, you know, we all lived together. I found that place on my trip to Poland. I found it. And we all lived in there.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long you stay in Krakow for?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Krakow we stayed for nine months. And yeah, the reason why we stayed in Krakow for nine months, because and we were going to go to Israel, to Palestine. But there was no possibility of going to Palestine because you couldn't get any documents. The English didn't allow you to get in, and we were not going to go on a ship, you know, and jump three miles from the shore and swim to Palestine. There was no question about it. With two women with three children.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, we were going to fix up documents to go to Australia. And we immediately wrote to my uncle here, Lester, which was Leisz, actually, in Poland. And then he guaranteed that he's going to give us work and he's going to look after us when we are in Australia until we, you know, work and whatever. And it took me nine months to get the documentation to go Australia.

speaker: interviewee
text: And there was one-- nine months, because we had to have like this-- exit visa from Poland, go through three zones in Germany. There was the British, American, and French.

speaker: interviewer
text: That was not so bad, but we had to get the entrance visa to go through France, which was a hell of a job, because they wouldn't give you a visa to go to Paris unless you had booked something to leave the country within three months, right? Either ship or-- you had to have documents, and you have to have tickets to go further, because they didn't want you there, right? So that took nine months. And one of those trips I remember very clearly, which was also very tragic. Have I got time to tell you that story now or not yet? No, we'll save it, I think, until the end. What date was it when finally you got your visas?

speaker: interviewee
text: We got the visas-- it was somewhere in-- I think it was in April, because in June we left Paris.

speaker: interviewer
text: I see. So, in June of 1946?

speaker: interviewee
text: In June '47.

speaker: interviewer
text: June '47. Pardon me. You left from--

speaker: interviewee
